planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination.

directions: Sow seeds directly into dry, sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in fall, pressing seed into the surface without covering, as light aids germination. Cold stratification over winter triggers reliable spring germination and establishment. Once seedlings emerge in spring, thin to 12-18 inches apart and avoid supplemental water once plants are established. Excellent for longleaf pine understory restorations and sandy native wildflower gardens in zones 6-9, where its loose, airy panicles of white to pale pink, lavender-marked flowers bloom in April and May.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Flora of North America Vol. 17 — Penstemon laxiflorus

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into dry, well-drained ground; cold-moist stratification over winter promotes reliable spring germination.

directions: Scatter seed in fall over dry, calcareous or sandy, well-drained soil in full sun, pressing lightly into the soil surface and allowing winter cold to break dormancy naturally. Distinct cupped (perfoliate) leaves clasp the stem, making seedlings easy to identify and protect during thinning to 12-18 inches apart in spring. Plants bloom with vivid scarlet-red, tubular flowers from April through June on tall stems reaching 2-4 feet, drawing hummingbirds throughout the blooming season. Thrives with no supplemental irrigation once established; ideal for Texas Hill Country, post oak savanna, and south-central wildflower gardens in zones 6-9.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Flora of North America Vol. 17 — Penstemon murrayanus

planting.note: Sow in fall directly onto rocky, calcareous, well-drained soil; seeds require winter cold stratification for spring germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall onto extremely rocky, thin, alkaline, calcareous soil in full sun, mimicking the cedar glade and limestone outcrop habitats of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau where this species is native. Avoid any soil amendment or organic enrichment, as lean, fast-draining conditions are essential to long-term survival and prevent root rot. Plants form compact, mat-like rosettes of narrow, evergreen leaves and send up upright flowering stems bearing dense spikes of violet-blue to lavender, hairy-throated flowers in spring. One of the most drought-tolerant native penstemons available; excellent in rock gardens and dry limestone garden settings in zones 6-9.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Diggs, Lipscomb & O'Kennon — Illustrated Flora of North Central Texas

planting.note: Sow directly in fall onto dry, rocky, calcareous soil; winter cold stratification supports reliable spring emergence.

directions: An endemic Texas penstemon restricted to rocky limestone outcrops and cedar glade openings of the Edwards Plateau; direct sow in fall onto very well-drained, rocky, alkaline soil in full sun and allow winter cold to stratify seed naturally. The flowers are arranged in distinctive whorls of three at each stem node — a reliable identifying trait — and emerge in brilliant magenta-red to carmine in April through June, attracting hummingbirds. Thin established seedlings to 12-18 inches and provide no supplemental water after the first season. An underused, striking native penstemon for Texas limestone gardens and dry, sunny borders in zones 6-9.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Flora of North America Vol. 17 — Penstemon triflorus

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost onto dry, lean soil in full sun; seed requires light to germinate — press onto surface without covering.

directions: Direct sow at last frost onto dry, lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun, pressing seed lightly onto the surface without covering since light improves germination. Thin seedlings to 12-18 inches once established and avoid any fertilizer or excess moisture, which promotes rank, floppy growth at the expense of flowering. Whorled clusters of purple to lavender tubular flowers with pale, purple-spotted bracts appear in summer and attract native bees, bumblebees, and hummingbirds; the whole plant releases a strong, spicy-mint fragrance when touched. Allow seed heads to ripen and scatter in place for reliable self-sowing in open, disturbed, sunny ground in subsequent years.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Diggs, Lipscomb & O'Kennon — Illustrated Flora of North Central Texas
